data.content:
### Setup Node.js Environment with GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

Uses the actions/setup-node action to install a specified version of Node.js on the runner. This example installs Node.js version 20 and adds both node and npm commands to the PATH, enabling Node.js package management and script execution in subsequent workflow steps.

```yaml
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: '20'
```

--------------------------------

### Setup Node.js Environment in Workflow

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

Installs a specific Node.js version (20 in this case) on the runner using `actions/setup-node@v4`. This action ensures that both `node` and `npm` commands are available in the system's PATH for subsequent workflow steps.

```yaml
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
```

--------------------------------

### Install Ruby dependencies with Bundler and setup-ruby action

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/ruby

Demonstrates basic Ruby workflow setup using the setup-ruby action to automatically install Bundler and dependencies. The action determines the Bundler version from gemfile.lock and installs the latest compatible version if not specified. This provides a simple one-command dependency installation.

```yaml
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v5
- uses: ruby/setup-ruby@ec02537da5712d66d4d50a0f33b7eb52773b5ed1
  with:
    ruby-version: '3.1'
- run: bundle install
```

--------------------------------

### Install Runner Scale Set with Helm

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/managing-self-hosted-runners-with-actions-runner-controller/quickstart-for-actions-runner-controller

Installs the GitHub Actions Runner Controller Helm chart with configuration for your repository, organization, or enterprise. Requires setting installation name, namespace, GitHub config URL, and personal access token (PAT) with appropriate scopes. The command creates the namespace if it doesn't exist and deploys the latest version of the gha-runner-scale-set chart.

```bash
INSTALLATION_NAME="arc-runner-set"
NAMESPACE="arc-runners"
GITHUB_CONFIG_URL="https://github.com/<your_enterprise/org/repo>"
GITHUB_PAT="<PAT>"
helm install "${INSTALLATION_NAME}" \
    --namespace "${NAMESPACE}" \
    --create-namespace \
    --set githubConfigUrl="${GITHUB_CONFIG_URL}" \
    --set githubConfigSecret.github_token="${GITHUB_PAT}" \
    oci://ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner-controller-charts/gha-runner-scale-set
```

--------------------------------

### Install Bats Testing Package with npm

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

Uses the `run` keyword in a GitHub Actions step to execute a shell command, installing the `bats` software testing package globally via npm. This action is crucial for setting up testing environments.

```yaml
- run: npm install -g bats
```

--------------------------------

### Define Basic Push Event Workflow in GitHub Actions (YAML)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

This YAML snippet configures a GitHub Actions workflow that automatically triggers on `push` events to the repository. It defines a job named `check-bats-version` which runs on `ubuntu-latest`, checks out the code, installs Node.js v20, globally installs the `bats` testing framework, and finally runs `bats -v` to confirm the installation. This workflow demonstrates a simple CI setup for a repository.

```yaml
name: learn-github-actions
run-name: ${{ github.actor }} is learning GitHub Actions
on: [push]
jobs:
  check-bats-version:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm install -g bats
      - run: bats -v
```

--------------------------------

### Runner Container Hooks Repository Setup

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/managing-self-hosted-runners/customizing-the-containers-used-by-jobs

Instructions for cloning the actions/runner-container-hooks repository and building customization scripts for Docker and Kubernetes environments. Includes example JSON configurations for prepare_job, run_script_step, and run_container_step.

```APIDOC
## Runner Container Hooks Repository Setup

### Description
Guide for setting up customization scripts using the GitHub Actions runner-container-hooks repository, with examples for Docker and Kubernetes deployments.

### Setup Steps

#### Step 1: Clone Repository
Clone the actions/runner-container-hooks repository to your self-hosted runner.

#### Step 2: Review Examples
The `examples/` directory contains customization command templates:
- `prepare_job.json` - Job preparation customization
- `run_script_step.json` - Script step customization
- `run_container_step.json` - Container step customization

Review these examples as starting points for your own customization commands.

#### Step 3: Build NPM Packages
Build the npm packages to generate `index.js` files in distribution directories:

```bash
npm install && npm run bootstrap && npm run build-all
```

This command generates:
- `packages/docker/dist/index.js`
- `packages/k8s/dist/index.js`

### Triggering the Generated Script
When the resulting `index.js` is triggered by GitHub Actions, it will run the customization commands defined in your JSON files. To trigger the `index.js`, add it to your `ACTIONS_RUNNER_CONTAINER_HOOKS` environment variable.

### Notes
- The resulting scripts are available for testing purposes
- Verify scripts meet your requirements before production use
- Scripts execute in the security context of the runner service account
```

--------------------------------

### Setup specific .NET version using semantic versioning

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/net

Configure a GitHub Actions job to use a specific version of .NET using semantic version syntax. This example sets up the latest minor release of .NET 6 using the setup-dotnet action, which ensures consistent behavior across different runners.

```yaml
    - name: Setup .NET 6.x
      uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
      with:
        # Semantic version range syntax or exact version of a dotnet version
        dotnet-version: '6.x'
```

--------------------------------

### Install Actions Runner Controller using Helm

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/managing-self-hosted-runners-with-actions-runner-controller/quickstart-for-actions-runner-controller

This command installs the Actions Runner Controller (ARC) operator and its Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) into a specified Kubernetes namespace using Helm. It creates the namespace if it doesn't exist and pulls the chart from the GitHub Container Registry. This example installs the latest version.

```bash
NAMESPACE="arc-systems"
helm install arc \
    --namespace "${NAMESPACE}" \
    --create-namespace \
    oci://ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner-controller-charts/gha-runner-scale-set-controller
```

--------------------------------

### Verify Helm Installation Status

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/managing-self-hosted-runners-with-actions-runner-controller/quickstart-for-actions-runner-controller

Lists all Helm releases across all namespaces to verify that the runner scale set and controller have been successfully deployed. Displays release name, namespace, revision, update timestamp, deployment status, chart version, and app version.

```bash
helm list -A
```

--------------------------------

### Build and Test Go Applications in GitHub Actions Workflow

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/go

This GitHub Actions workflow defines a job to build and test a Go application. It checks out the repository, sets up Go using `setup-go` for version `1.21.x`, installs dependencies with `go get .`, builds the project with `go build -v ./...`, and finally runs tests using `go test`.

```YAML
name: Go
on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - name: Setup Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: '1.21.x'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: go get .
      - name: Build
        run: go build -v ./...
      - name: Test with the Go CLI
        run: go test

```

--------------------------------

### Configure Docker Container Step with Dockerfile in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/managing-self-hosted-runners/customizing-the-containers-used-by-jobs

Specifies a container execution step using a Dockerfile with configured volumes, environment variables, and resource constraints. This example demonstrates how to use the 'dockerfile' parameter to reference a custom Dockerfile, configure system and user mount volumes, set environment variables, and map ports. The configuration includes job container state, networking setup, and entry point specification.

```json
{
  "command": "run_container_step",
  "responseFile": null,
  "state": {
    "network": "example_network_53269bd575972817b43f7733536b200c",
    "jobContainer": "82e8219701fe096a35941d869cf3d71af1d943b5d8bdd718857fb87ac3042480",
    "services": {
      "redis": "60972d9aa486605e66b0dad4abb678dc3d9116f536579e418176eedb8abb9105"
    }
  },
  "args": {
    "image": null,
    "dockerfile": "/__w/_actions/foo/dockerfile",
    "entryPointArgs": ["hello world"],
    "entryPoint": "echo",
    "workingDirectory": "/__w/octocat-test2/octocat-test2",
    "createOptions": "--cpus 1",
    "environmentVariables": {
      "NODE_ENV": "development"
    },
    "prependPath": ["/foo/bar", "bar/foo"],
    "userMountVolumes": [
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "my_docker_volume",
        "targetVolumePath": "/volume_mount",
        "readOnly": false
      }
    ],
    "systemMountVolumes": [
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/_work",
        "targetVolumePath": "/__w",
        "readOnly": false
      },
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/externals",
        "targetVolumePath": "/__e",
        "readOnly": true
      },
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/_work/_temp",
        "targetVolumePath": "/__w/_temp",
        "readOnly": false
      },
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/_work/_actions",
        "targetVolumePath": "/__w/_actions",
        "readOnly": false
      },
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/_work/_tool",
        "targetVolumePath": "/__w/_tool",
        "readOnly": false
      },
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/_work/_temp/_github_home",
        "targetVolumePath": "/github/home",
        "readOnly": false
      },
      {
        "sourceVolumePath": "/home/octocat/git/runner/_layout/_work/_temp/_github_workflow",
        "targetVolumePath": "/github/workflow",
        "readOnly": false
      }
    ],
    "registry": null,
    "portMappings": {
      "80": "801"
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Travis CI Node.js Build and Test Phases

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/migrating-to-github-actions/manually-migrating-to-github-actions/migrating-from-travis-ci-to-github-actions

Shows Travis CI configuration for Node.js projects with install and script phases. This example demonstrates npm dependency installation and build/test execution using Travis CI's phase-based approach. GitHub Actions replaces this with action-based steps.

```yaml
install:
  - npm install
script:
  - npm run build
  - npm test
```

--------------------------------

### Set up Ruby and Run Bundle Commands in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/ruby

Basic GitHub Actions workflow that checks out code, sets up a specific Ruby version using the ruby/setup-ruby action, installs dependencies with bundle, and runs tests with rake. This ensures consistent Ruby environment setup across different runners.

```yaml
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v5
- uses: ruby/setup-ruby@ec02537da5712d66d4d50a0f33b7eb52773b5ed1
  with:
    ruby-version: '3.1' # Not needed with a .ruby-version file
- run: bundle install
- run: bundle exec rake
```

--------------------------------

### Install Bats Package within Workflow Steps

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

Executes `npm install -g bats` as a step within the job. This command installs the `bats` software testing package globally on the runner, making it ready for use in subsequent testing steps.

```yaml
      - run: npm install -g bats
```

--------------------------------

### Install NuGet dependencies in GitHub Actions workflow

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/net

Install NuGet package dependencies using the dotnet CLI within a GitHub Actions workflow. This example sets up .NET 6.0.x and installs the Newtonsoft.Json package version 12.0.1, which is essential for building and testing .NET projects.

```yaml
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v5
- name: Setup dotnet
  uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
  with:
    dotnet-version: '6.0.x'
- name: Install dependencies
  run: dotnet add package Newtonsoft.Json --version 12.0.1
```

--------------------------------

### Install Go dependencies in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/go

This YAML snippet illustrates how to install Go project dependencies within a GitHub Actions workflow. It first checks out the repository, sets up Go version '1.21.x', and then uses `go get` commands to fetch dependencies, including specific modules and versions.

```yaml
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - name: Setup Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: '1.21.x'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          go get .
          go get example.com/octo-examplemodule
          go get example.com/octo-examplemodule@v1.3.4
```

--------------------------------

### Example output for Bamboo deployment project migration to GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/migrate-to-github-actions/automated-migrations/bamboo-migration

This example illustrates the successful outcome of running the `gh actions-importer migrate bamboo deployment` command. It displays the location of the log file and the direct link to the pull request created with the converted GitHub Actions workflow.

```bash
$ gh actions-importer migrate bamboo deployment --deployment-project-id 123 --target-url https://github.com/octo-org/octo-repo --output-dir tmp/migrate
[2023-04-20 22:08:20] Logs: 'tmp/migrate/log/actions-importer-20230420-014033.log'
[2023-04-20 22:08:20] Pull request: 'https://github.com/octo-org/octo-repo/pull/1'
```

--------------------------------

### Verify Bats Version within Workflow Steps

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

Runs the `bats -v` command as a final step in the job. This verifies the successful installation and accessibility of the `bats` testing framework by printing its version to the console.

```yaml
      - run: bats -v
```

--------------------------------

### GitHub Actions Repository Syntax Examples

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/secure-use-reference

Examples of supported GitHub Actions reference syntax that Dependabot can update. Includes semantic versioning with tags and commit-based references. Dependabot only supports GitHub repository syntax and ignores locally referenced actions or Docker syntax references.

```yaml
actions/checkout@v5
```

```yaml
actions/checkout@<commit>
```

```yaml
actions/checkout@<commit> #<tag or link>
```

```yaml
actions/checkout@<tag> #<tag or link>
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Caching for CI/CD Workflows

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/migrating-to-github-actions/manually-migrating-to-github-actions/migrating-from-gitlab-cicd-to-github-actions

This demonstrates how to implement caching in CI/CD pipelines to speed up dependency installation and build times. Examples for GitLab CI/CD and GitHub Actions show how to define cache keys, paths, and restoration logic for faster workflow execution.

```yaml
image: node:latest\n\ncache:\n  key: $CI_COMMIT_REF_SLUG\n  paths:\n    - .npm/\n\nbefore_script:\n  - npm ci --cache .npm --prefer-offline\n\ntest_async:\n  script:\n    - node ./specs/start.js ./specs/async.spec.js\n
```

```yaml
jobs:\n  test_async:\n    runs-on: ubuntu-latest\n    steps:\n    - name: Cache node modules\n      uses: actions/cache@v4\n      with:\n        path: ~/.npm\n        key: v1-npm-deps-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}\n        restore-keys: v1-npm-deps-\n
```

--------------------------------

### Permissions Configuration Examples

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

Practical examples of configuring GITHUB_TOKEN permissions for common workflows and use cases in GitHub Actions.

```APIDOC
## GITHUB_TOKEN Permissions Configuration Examples

### Example 1: Minimal Read-Only Permissions
**Use Case**: Read repository contents without modification

```yaml
permissions:
  contents: read
```

---

### Example 2: Release Creation Workflow
**Use Case**: Create releases and manage repository contents

```yaml
permissions:
  contents: write
  actions: read
```

---

### Example 3: Pull Request Automation
**Use Case**: Automate pull request tasks like labeling and commenting

```yaml
permissions:
  pull-requests: write
  contents: read
  issues: write
```

---

### Example 4: Code Scanning Workflow
**Use Case**: Update code scanning alert status

```yaml
permissions:
  security-events: write
  contents: read
```

---

### Example 5: Deployment Workflow
**Use Case**: Create and manage deployments

```yaml
permissions:
  deployments: write
  contents: read
  statuses: write
```

---

### Example 6: Multi-Permission Workflow
**Use Case**: Comprehensive workflow with multiple permission needs

```yaml
permissions:
  actions: read
  contents: write
  issues: write
  pull-requests: write
  packages: write
  pages: write
```

---

### Example 7: Disable All Permissions
**Use Case**: Restrict all access except read-only

```yaml
permissions:
  actions: none
  contents: none
  issues: none
  pull-requests: none
```

---

### Example 8: Job-Level Permissions
**Use Case**: Different permissions for different jobs

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    permissions:
      contents: read
  deploy:
    permissions:
      deployments: write
      contents: read
  notify:
    permissions:
      issues: write
```
```

--------------------------------

### Example .npmrc file content for private registry authentication

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-builds-and-tests/building-and-testing-nodejs

This is an example of the `.npmrc` file content generated by the `setup-node` action when configuring a private npm registry with authentication and a package scope. It shows how the authentication token and registry settings are structured for npm.

```ini
//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=${NODE_AUTH_TOKEN}
@octocat:registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/
always-auth=true

```

--------------------------------

### jobs.<job_id>.services.<service_id>.image

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

Specifies the Docker image to use for a service container. Can be a Docker Hub image name or a registry name. An empty string will prevent the service from starting, allowing for conditional service setup.

```APIDOC
## Configuration Property jobs.<job_id>.services.<service_id>.image

### Description
The Docker image to use as the service container to run the action. The value can be the Docker Hub image name or a registry name. If `jobs.<job_id>.services.<service_id>.image` is assigned an empty string, the service will not start. You can use this to set up conditional services.

### Method
N/A (Configuration Property)

### Endpoint
jobs.<job_id>.services.<service_id>.image

### Parameters
#### Request Body
- **image** (string) - Required - The Docker image name or registry path.

### Request Example
```json
{
  "services": {
    "nginx": {
      "image": "${{ options.nginx == true && 'nginx' || '' }}"
    }
  }
}
```

### Response
Not applicable.
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Dynamic Workflow Run Name

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-an-example-workflow

Specifies a custom name for each workflow run, displayed in the 'Actions' tab. This example utilizes the `github` context to dynamically include the username of the workflow's triggerer, enhancing traceability.

```yaml
run-name: ${{ github.actor }} is learning GitHub Actions
```

--------------------------------

### Configure GitHub Actions Importer with Example Output

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/migrate-to-github-actions/automated-migrations/bitbucket-pipelines-migration

Example execution of the `configure` command showing the interactive prompts and successful configuration output. Demonstrates entering GitHub personal access token, GitHub instance URL, Bitbucket workspace access token, and Bitbucket instance URL.

```bash
$ gh actions-importer configure
✔ Which CI providers are you configuring?: Bitbucket
Enter the following values (leave empty to omit):
✔ Personal access token for GitHub: ***************
✔ Base url of the GitHub instance: https://github.com
✔ Personal access token for Bitbucket: ********************
✔ Base url of the Bitbucket instance: https://bitbucket.example.com
Environment variables successfully updated.
```

--------------------------------

### Example output for migrating an Azure DevOps release pipeline

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/migrate-to-github-actions/automated-migrations/azure-devops-migration

This code block shows an example of the successful output from the `gh actions-importer migrate` command for a release pipeline. It indicates the path to the detailed logs and the URL of the newly created pull request on GitHub, which contains the converted workflow.

```bash
$ gh actions-importer migrate azure-devops release --target-url https://github.com/octo-org/octo-repo --output-dir tmp/migrate --azure-devops-project my-azure-devops-project
[2022-08-20 22:08:20] Logs: 'tmp/migrate/log/actions-importer-20220916-014033.log'
[2022-08-20 22:08:20] Pull request: 'https://github.com/octo-org/octo-repo/pull/1'
```

--------------------------------

### Example output for migrating an Azure DevOps build pipeline

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/migrate-to-github-actions/automated-migrations/azure-devops-migration

This code block shows an example of the successful output from the `gh actions-importer migrate` command for a build pipeline. It indicates the path to the detailed logs and the URL of the newly created pull request on GitHub, which contains the converted workflow.

```bash
$ gh actions-importer migrate azure-devops pipeline --target-url https://github.com/octo-org/octo-repo --output-dir tmp/migrate --azure-devops-project my-azure-devops-project
[2022-08-20 22:08:20] Logs: 'tmp/migrate/log/actions-importer-20220916-014033.log'
[2022-08-20 22:08:20] Pull request: 'https://github.com/octo-org/octo-repo/pull/1'
```

--------------------------------

### Set Workflow-Level Concurrency in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This example demonstrates how to apply concurrency control at the workflow level to limit the number of concurrent workflow runs. It defines a concurrency group based on the workflow name and Git reference, and ensures that any in-progress runs in the same group are cancelled when a new one starts.

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

```

--------------------------------

### Configure GitHub Actions Job Routing with Runner Groups

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/larger-runners/use-larger-runners_platform=windows

YAML workflow configuration demonstrating how to route jobs to specific runner groups using the `runs-on` key with the `group` property. This example sends jobs to any available runner in the `ubuntu-runners` group, which must be pre-configured by organization administrators. The workflow includes Node.js setup and bats installation as example steps.

```yaml
name: learn-github-actions
on: [push]
jobs:
  check-bats-version:
    runs-on:
      group: ubuntu-runners
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '14'
      - run: npm install -g bats
      - run: bats -v
```

--------------------------------

### Install Homebrew packages and casks on macOS runner

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/github-hosted-runners/customize-runners

Demonstrates installing both Homebrew packages and casks on a macOS-latest GitHub-hosted runner. Example shows installing GitHub CLI as a package and Microsoft Edge as a cask with brew update before each installation.

```yaml
name: Build on macOS
on: push

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: macos-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check out repository code
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - name: Install GitHub CLI
        run: |
          brew update
          brew install gh
      - name: Install Microsoft Edge
        run: |
          brew update
          brew install --cask microsoft-edge
```

--------------------------------

### Create GitHub Actions Workflow with Runner Scale Set

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/managing-self-hosted-runners-with-actions-runner-controller/quickstart-for-actions-runner-controller

A GitHub Actions workflow that uses runner scale set runners via the Actions Runner Controller. The workflow is triggered manually and runs a simple echo command on the arc-runner-set runners. The runs-on value must match the Helm installation name from the ARC setup.

```yaml
name: Actions Runner Controller Demo
on:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  Explore-GitHub-Actions:
    # You need to use the INSTALLATION_NAME from the previous step
    runs-on: arc-runner-set
    steps:
    - run: echo "🎉 This job uses runner scale set runners!"
```

--------------------------------

### Build and Test .NET Code with dotnet CLI

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/net

GitHub Actions workflow steps that execute dotnet build and dotnet test commands. Demonstrates checking out code, setting up .NET SDK version 6.0.x, restoring dependencies, building the project, and running tests. Useful for CI/CD pipelines requiring standard .NET build and test verification.

```yaml
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v5
- name: Setup dotnet
  uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
  with:
    dotnet-version: '6.0.x'
- name: Install dependencies
  run: dotnet restore
- name: Build
  run: dotnet build --no-restore
- name: Test with the dotnet CLI
  run: dotnet test --no-build
```

--------------------------------

### Install Node.js dependencies using npm ci in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-builds-and-tests/building-and-testing-nodejs

This GitHub Actions workflow step installs Node.js project dependencies using `npm ci`. It ensures a clean, locked installation based on `package-lock.json` or `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, preventing updates to the lock file and offering faster, more reliable builds. This snippet requires `actions/checkout` and `actions/setup-node` to be used.

```yaml
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v5
- name: Use Node.js
  uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: '20.x'
- name: Install dependencies
  run: npm ci

```

### Install GitHub Actions Toolkit Packages (npm)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/creating-actions/creating-a-javascript-action

Installs essential Node.js packages from the GitHub Actions Toolkit: `@actions/core` and `@actions/github`. These packages provide APIs for interacting with workflow commands, handling inputs/outputs, and accessing GitHub context within a JavaScript action.

```Shell
npm install @actions/core @actions/github
```

### Configure Pre-entrypoint Script for GitHub Actions Docker

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/creating-actions/metadata-syntax-for-github-actions

Demonstrates how to specify a script to run before the main 'entrypoint' of a Docker-based GitHub Action. This script executes in a new container, so state must be managed via the workspace, HOME, or 'STATE_' variables. The example uses 'setup.sh' as the pre-entrypoint script.

```yaml
runs:
  using: 'docker'
  image: 'Dockerfile'
  args:
    - 'bzz'
  pre-entrypoint: 'setup.sh'
  entrypoint: 'main.sh'
```

### Install PowerShell Dependencies from Gallery in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/powershell

A GitHub Actions job that installs multiple PowerShell modules (SqlServer and PSScriptAnalyzer) from the PowerShell Gallery with trusted repository configuration. This example shows how to set the installation policy and use Install-Module for dependency management in CI workflows.

```yaml
jobs:
  install-dependencies:
    name: Install dependencies
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - name: Install from PSGallery
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          Set-PSRepository PSGallery -InstallationPolicy Trusted
          Install-Module SqlServer, PSScriptAnalyzer
```

### GitHub Actions Workflow for Apple App Signing with Certificates and Provisioning Profiles

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/deploy/deploy-to-third-party-platforms/sign-xcode-applications

This GitHub Actions workflow demonstrates how to set up a macOS runner to build an Apple application. It includes steps to check out the repository and, crucially, to import Base64-encoded Apple certificates and provisioning profiles from repository secrets. The process involves creating a temporary keychain, importing the certificate, and applying the provisioning profile, preparing the environment for app signing. It requires secrets for `BUILD_CERTIFICATE_BASE64`, `P12_PASSWORD`, `BUILD_PROVISION_PROFILE_BASE64`, and `KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD`.

```yaml
name: App build
on: push

jobs:
  build_with_signing:
    runs-on: macos-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - name: Install the Apple certificate and provisioning profile
        env:
          BUILD_CERTIFICATE_BASE64: ${{ secrets.BUILD_CERTIFICATE_BASE64 }}
          P12_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.P12_PASSWORD }}
          BUILD_PROVISION_PROFILE_BASE64: ${{ secrets.BUILD_PROVISION_PROFILE_BASE64 }}
          KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD }}
        run: |
          # create variables
          CERTIFICATE_PATH=$RUNNER_TEMP/build_certificate.p12
          PP_PATH=$RUNNER_TEMP/build_pp.mobileprovision
          KEYCHAIN_PATH=$RUNNER_TEMP/app-signing.keychain-db

          # import certificate and provisioning profile from secrets
          echo -n "$BUILD_CERTIFICATE_BASE64" | base64 --decode -o $CERTIFICATE_PATH
          echo -n "$BUILD_PROVISION_PROFILE_BASE64" | base64 --decode -o $PP_PATH

          # create temporary keychain
          security create-keychain -p "$KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD" $KEYCHAIN_PATH
          security set-keychain-settings -lut 21600 $KEYCHAIN_PATH
          security unlock-keychain -p "$KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD" $KEYCHAIN_PATH

          # import certificate to keychain
          security import $CERTIFICATE_PATH -P "$P12_PASSWORD" -A -t cert -f pkcs12 -k $KEYCHAIN_PATH
          security set-key-partition-list -S apple-tool:,apple: -k "$KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD" $KEYCHAIN_PATH
          security list-keychain -d user -s $KEYCHAIN_PATH

          # apply provisioning profile
          mkdir -p ~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning\ Profiles
          cp $PP_PATH ~/Library/MobileDevice/Provisioning\ Profiles
      - name: Build app
          # ...

```

### Start Just-In-Time (JIT) Runner with Configuration

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/secure-use-reference

This command demonstrates how to launch a self-hosted runner configured for just-in-time (JIT) operation. The `encoded_jit_config` variable, typically obtained from the GitHub REST API, contains the necessary registration and configuration details, ensuring the runner performs a single job before automatic removal, enhancing security.

```shell
./run.sh --jitconfig ${encoded_jit_config}
```

This text-based structure shows the recommended directory layout for a repository containing both workflow files and local actions. The hello-world-action is defined in .github/actions/ and referenced in the workflow file.

```text
|-- hello-world (repository)
|   |__ .github
|       └── workflows
|           └── my-first-workflow.yml
|       └── actions
|           |__ hello-world-action
|               └── action.yml
```

### GitHub Actions Filter Pattern Examples for Branches and Tags

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-your-workflow-with-github-actions/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

Provides valid YAML syntax examples for using filter patterns with special characters in GitHub Actions workflows. Demonstrates proper quoting requirements for patterns starting with special characters like `*`, `[`, or `!` to prevent YAML parse errors.

```yaml
# Valid
paths:
  - '**/README.md'

# Invalid - creates a parse error that
# prevents your workflow from running.
paths:
  - **/README.md

# Valid
branches: [ main, 'release/v[0-9].[0-9]' ]

# Invalid - creates a parse error
branches: [ main, release/v[0-9].[0-9] ]
```

### Build and Test Java Maven Project with Verify Phase in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-builds-and-tests/building-and-testing-java-with-maven

This GitHub Actions YAML snippet demonstrates how to build and test a Java project using Maven's `verify` phase. It first sets up JDK 17 using the `setup-java` action and then executes the `mvn --batch-mode --update-snapshots verify` command, which downloads dependencies, builds classes, runs tests, and ensures project integrity.

```yaml
steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@v5
  - uses: actions/setup-java@v4
    with:
      java-version: '17'
      distribution: 'temurin'
  - name: Run the Maven verify phase
    run: mvn --batch-mode --update-snapshots verify
```

kubectl get pods -n arc-systems

### Configure Swift CI workflow with multiple versions and OS matrix

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/build-and-test-code/swift

Sets up a GitHub Actions workflow to build and test a Swift package across multiple Swift versions (5.2, 5.3) and operating systems (Ubuntu and macOS). Uses the swift-actions/setup-swift action to configure the specified Swift version, then runs build and test commands. The matrix strategy automatically creates multiple job runs for each combination of OS and Swift version.

```yaml
name: Swift

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    name: Swift ${{ matrix.swift }} on ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest]
        swift: ["5.2", "5.3"]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    steps:
      - uses: swift-actions/setup-swift@65540b95f51493d65f5e59e97dcef9629ddf11bf
        with:
          swift-version: ${{ matrix.swift }}
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
      - name: Build
        run: swift build
      - name: Run tests
        run: swift test
```
